subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can tell which of two objects covers more space (has a bigger area).
Your child can match one object to one other object, like pairing socks.
Your child can tell the difference between a whole object and half of that object.
Your child can tell the difference between an object that takes up space (like a box) and one that does not (like a piece of paper).
Your child can tell if a group of objects are all the same or if some are different.
Your child can finish a simple pattern that follows a rule, like 2 red blocks for every 1 blue block.
Your child understands when a group has been split into smaller groups that are all the same size.
Your child can spot a shape that has been split into equal parts.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Early Measurement and Sorting — Area, Equivalence, Patterns, and Classification
coherence_reason: The standards cover several distinct early math concepts: area comparison, one-to-one correspondence, part-whole relationships, volume vs. area, sorting/classification, patterning, equal grouping, and fractions, which are not a single coherent topic.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child knows a statistical question expects different answers because the data can vary.
Your child knows that a good statistical question expects different answers and includes that in how it is answered.
Your child can pick questions that will give number answers from a list of questions.
Your child can ask questions that need numbers to answer.
Your child understands that a statistical question expects different answers and includes this variation when answering.
Identify a question.
Your child knows what a question mark (?) means in a question.
Your child understands the difference between a regular question and a question that needs data to answer.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Statistics — Statistical Questions and Variability
coherence_reason: Most standards are about understanding and identifying statistical questions, but a few touch on general question identification and punctuation, which is a minor variation.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can name parts of an expression like sum, product, factor, and coefficient, and see parts of an expression as one piece.
Your child can name parts of math expressions like sum, product, and factor, and see parts of an expression as one unit.
Your child can name parts of an expression like terms, coefficients, sums, and products, and see parts of an expression as one piece.
Your child can name parts of math expressions using terms like sum, product, factor, and coefficient.
Algebraic Concepts
Your child can find like terms in an algebra expression.
Your child can identify parts of a math expression and understand that some parts can be grouped together as one.
Your child can recognize and explain words like equation, variable, expression, term, and coefficient.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Algebraic Expressions — Parts of an Expression
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on identifying and naming parts of expressions, with some minor inclusion of related algebraic vocabulary and finding like terms.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Attend to precision.
Your child uses the right ways to present and work with others to complete tasks well.
Collaboration
Career Research
Your child shows interest and participates actively in learning about coordinate grids.
Your child shows interest and participates actively when learning about expressions.
Your child shows interest and participates in learning about data.
Your child shows interest and stays involved while learning about shapes and volume.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Mathematical Practices — Precision, Collaboration, and Engagement
coherence_reason: The standards mix mathematical practice standards (precision, collaboration) with student engagement/participation across several unrelated math content areas (coordinate grids, expressions, data, shapes/volume).
split_topics: Student Engagement in Math Content (coordinate grids, expressions, data, geometry/volume)

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use ratio thinking to change measurement units and correctly adjust units when multiplying or dividing.
Your child can solve problems about unit rates, like figuring out prices per item or speeds.
Your child can show a simple ratio, like 1 cat for every 2 dogs.
Your child understands what a ratio is and can use words to explain the relationship between two amounts.
Your child can make tables of equivalent ratios with whole numbers, find missing numbers, plot these on graphs, and use tables to compare ratios.
Your child can compare two simple fractions with 1 on top, like 1/4 and 1/6.
Your child understands ratios and can use ratio thinking to solve problems.
Your child can find a percent of a number by thinking of it as parts per 100 and solve problems where they find the whole from a part and a percent.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Ratios and Proportional Relationships — Understanding and Applying Ratios
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on understanding and applying ratios, unit rates, and percent, but one standard on comparing simple fractions could be considered a separate subtopic.
split_topics: Understanding and using ratios, rates, and percent

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.
Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.
Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.
Your child can write interesting stories using sensory details, dialogue, description, and clear sequencing.
Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.
Your child uses what they know about word parts and sentence structure to understand new words in what they read.
Your child can organize and share their thoughts and opinions clearly.
Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Mathematical Practices — Problem Solving and Reasoning
coherence_reason: Some standards focus on mathematical reasoning and problem solving, but several are about writing or reading skills, indicating two distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Model with mathematics.
Your child can make a graph or table that shows how data can change or vary.
Your child can make a graph or table that shows how data can be different.
Your child can use letters to show how two changing amounts are related, write equations for these, and understand the relationship by looking at graphs and tables.
Your child can show and study how one number changes when another number changes.
Your child can use numbers and objects to show a simple relationship, like 3 apples for every 2 oranges.
Your child can compare checking accounts and debit cards from different banks, including their features and costs.
Your child can keep track of money in a check register by recording deposits, withdrawals, and transfers.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Relationships — Graphs, Tables, and Real-World Modeling
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on modeling relationships using graphs, tables, and variables, but two standards are about financial literacy (bank accounts, check registers), which is a distinct real-world application.
split_topics: Modeling mathematical relationships with graphs, tables, and variables
Financial literacy: banking and check registers

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can tell which of two objects holds more or has a bigger volume.
Your child can solve real-life or math problems about how much space something takes up by using small cubes.

Your child can find the volume of a box-shaped figure with fractional side lengths by filling it with smaller cubes and by multiplying the side lengths, using formulas to solve real-world and math problems.
Your child can solve real-life or math problems about volume by using unit cubes.
Your child can solve problems that involve finding area, surface area, and volume in real-life and math situations.
Your child can solve math and real-world problems involving area, surface area, and volume.
Your child can use formulas to find the volume of prisms and describe 3D shapes like prisms and cylinders.
Your child can find the volume of a rectangular box using a picture model and the volume formula.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Geometry — Volume, Area, and Surface Area
coherence_reason: Most standards address volume of 3D shapes and solving volume problems, but several also include area and surface area, introducing a second concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can draw flat patterns made of rectangles and triangles that fold into 3D shapes, and use these patterns to find the total surface area of the shapes, solving real-life and math problems.
Your child can draw flat patterns (nets) of 3D shapes like prisms and use them to find surface area in real-life problems.
Your child can find the surface area of boxes and pyramids by using their flat shapes (nets).
Your child can find the surface area of a box shape by adding up the areas of all the flat shapes that make it up.
Your child can draw flat patterns (nets) of 3D shapes and use them to find surface area, solving real-world problems.
Your child can find the surface area of 3D shapes like triangular and rectangular boxes, including cubes, using given formulas.
Geometry
Your child can draw flat patterns made of rectangles and triangles that can be folded to make 3D shapes.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Geometry — Surface Area and Nets of 3D Shapes
coherence_reason: All standards focus on drawing and using nets to find the surface area of 3D shapes such as prisms and pyramids, with both real-world and mathematical contexts included.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can show number data on number lines using dot plots, histograms, and box plots.
Your child can look at a bar or circle graph and tell which of three amounts is the biggest.
Your child can describe what is being measured in data, how it was measured, and the units used.
Your child can describe if data on a graph is going up, going down, or staying the same using simple words.
Your child can tell how many data points were collected.
Your child can look at a bar or circle graph and tell which of three amounts is the smallest.
Your child can choose the best way to describe data based on its shape and the situation it came from.
Your child can explain what a graph or table shows about a set of data.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Data Analysis — Interpreting and Representing Data
coherence_reason: All standards focus on interpreting, representing, and describing data using various graphs, though there is minor variation between reading data and constructing/choosing representations.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can summarize sets of numbers by explaining what they mean in their real-life context.
Your child can write and explain order statements about rational numbers in real-world examples.
Your child can find and explain numbers that show the center and spread of data and describe patterns or unusual points.
Your child looks at how informational texts are organized to understand their meaning.
Your child can explain why people moved to cities and what happened because of this, both good and bad.
Your child learns about how data can vary and what that means.
Your child understands that one number can show the center of data and another number can show how much the data varies.
Your child can find which object or symbol shows up the most in a row of mixed items.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Statistics — Describing and Summarizing Data
coherence_reason: Most standards are about summarizing and describing data in statistics, but two standards are unrelated (one is reading comprehension and one is history/social studies).
split_topics: Social Studies — Urbanization Causes and Effects

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that the signs in ordered pairs show where points are on the coordinate plane and can see how points reflect across axes.
Your child understands how Alabama’s five geographic regions affected where settlers moved in the early 1800s.
Your child understands how the signs of numbers in pairs show locations in different parts of the coordinate plane and how changing signs reflects points across axes.
Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross to find and name points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.
Your child knows what it means for a function to be continuous, both by its definition and by looking at its graph.
Your child knows that signs in ordered pairs show locations on a graph and can see how changing signs reflects points across axes.
Your child understands the signs of numbers in each quadrant: (+,+), (+,-), (-,-), and (-,+).
Your child understands that a coordinate grid is made by crossing a vertical and a horizontal number line.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Coordinate Plane — Quadrants, Signs, and Reflections
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on coordinate plane concepts—signs, quadrants, ordered pairs, and reflections—but a few mention grids and number lines more generally.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find the greatest common factor of two numbers up to 100 and the least common multiple of two numbers up to 12, and use these to rewrite sums in simpler forms.
Your child can split a group of items into smaller groups that are all equal.
Your child can find and show a group with a certain number of objects.
Your child can work quickly with large numbers and find common factors and multiples.
Your child can find the greatest common factor and least common multiple of numbers and use the distributive property to rewrite sums with common factors.
Your child can work with large numbers easily and find common factors and multiples.
Your child can find the greatest common factor and least common multiple of two whole numbers.
Your child can use tools to find the greatest common factor and least common multiple of two numbers up to 50.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Factors and Multiples — Greatest Common Factor and Least Common Multiple
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on GCF and LCM, but a few mention general grouping or partitioning which is related but slightly different in emphasis.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can tell when two math sentences mean the same thing.
Your child can tell when two expressions are equal because they give the same result no matter what number is used.
Your child can use math rules to create expressions that mean the same thing.
Your child can use addition rules to find different ways to write the same number sentence.
Your child can spot number sentences that are equal to each other.
Your child can match a math sentence to the correct picture.
Your child can show that two groups of objects are equal and use the equal sign (=) to show they are the same.
Your child can tell when two math expressions are equal because they give the same answer no matter what number is used.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Expressions and Equations — Recognizing Equivalent Expressions
coherence_reason: All standards focus on determining when two mathematical expressions or sentences are equivalent, whether numerically or algebraically.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve real-life or math problems about area using unit squares.
Your child can find the area of a rectangle by counting the number of small squares inside it.

Your child can find the area of right triangles, other triangles, special four-sided shapes, and polygons by breaking them into rectangles or triangles, and use these methods to solve real-world and math problems.
Your child can solve real-life or math problems about area by using small squares.
Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into simpler shapes.
Your child can break down four-sided shapes and combined shapes into rectangles or triangles to find their area.
Your child can use a formula to find the area of a triangle.
Your child can find the area of shapes made of rectangles and triangles by breaking them apart.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Geometry — Area of Polygons by Decomposition
coherence_reason: All standards focus on finding the area of various polygons, particularly by decomposing them into rectangles and triangles, and applying these strategies to real-world and mathematical problems.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can understand and calculate division of fractions by fractions and solve related word problems using pictures and equations.
Your child can use what they know about multiplication and division to divide fractions by fractions.
Your child can solve simple multiplication problems using objects or a calculator.
Your child can solve multiplication problems with two numbers, where the answer is up to 50, using objects or a calculator.
Your child can solve simple multiplication problems (like 3 x 4) using objects or a calculator, with answers up to 15.
Your child can use what they know about numbers to understand and work with rational numbers.
Your child can solve a division problem by sharing things out equally.
Your child can quickly divide large numbers using the usual steps.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Fractions — Division of Fractions by Fractions
coherence_reason: Some standards focus specifically on dividing fractions by fractions, while others address basic multiplication, division of whole numbers, and understanding rational numbers, which are related but distinct concepts.
split_topics: Basic Multiplication and Division with Whole Numbers
Understanding and Working with Rational Numbers

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve real-life and math problems by writing and solving simple equations like x + p = q or px = q with positive numbers.
Your child can solve real-life and math problems by writing and solving simple equations like x + p = q or p × x = q.
Your child can solve problems by writing and solving simple equations like x + p = q or px = q with positive numbers.
Your child can solve problems by writing and solving simple equations like x + p = q or px = q when all numbers are nonnegative.
Your child can solve multiplication equations like p × x = q when p, q, and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.
Your child can solve problems by writing and solving simple equations like x plus a number equals another number, or a number times x equals another number.
Your child can solve addition equations like x + p = q when p, q, and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.
Your child can write an equation that shows how one amount depends on another using the form y = p times x, where all numbers are zero or positive and p is not zero.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Equations — Solving One-Step Addition and Multiplication Equations with Positive Numbers
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on solving simple one-step equations with positive numbers, but one standard also introduces writing equations to represent relationships (y = px), which is slightly broader than just solving equations.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands absolute value as how far a number is from zero on the number line and can explain this in real-life situations.
Your child understands how to order rational numbers and knows what absolute value means.
Your child understands how the War of 1812 and the Creek War affected Alabama socially, politically, and economically.
Your child understands why it’s important to build a good credit history.
Your child understands what rights people have and what they should do as citizens in your community and state.
Your child knows why credit reports are important for people who borrow money and for the lenders who loan it.
Your child understands absolute value as how far a number is from zero and can explain it in real-life situations.
Your child understands and uses the intermediate value and extreme value theorems to solve problems.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Rational Numbers — Absolute Value and Ordering
coherence_reason: The cluster contains absolute value and ordering rational numbers in math, but also unrelated standards about history, civics, and credit, which should be split.
split_topics: Math: Absolute Value and Ordering Rational Numbers
History: War of 1812 and Creek War Effects
Civics: Rights and Responsibilities of Citizens
Financial Literacy: Credit History and Reports
Math: Intermediate and Extreme Value Theorems

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that numbers above zero are positive and numbers below zero are negative.
Your child knows that positive and negative numbers describe things going in opposite directions or having opposite values and can explain what zero means in these situations.
Your child knows that opposite signs mean numbers are on opposite sides of zero and that the opposite of the opposite number is the original number.
Your child can show what 'more than' a number means using objects, and can take away objects until none are left.
Your child knows that positive and negative numbers are used together to talk about things like temperatures above or below zero.
Your child understands that positive and negative numbers help describe things that go in opposite directions, like above and below zero.
Your child understands that positive and negative numbers show opposite directions or values, like temperature above or below zero, and can explain what zero means in these cases.
Your child can show positive and negative numbers on a number line from real-life situations and explain what zero means in that situation.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Rational Numbers — Positive and Negative Numbers and Zero
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding positive and negative numbers, their opposites, the meaning of zero, and representing them in real-world contexts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can plot shapes on a grid using points and find the length of sides when points share the same x or y coordinate, applying this to solve real-world and math problems.
Your child can draw and identify points, lines, line segments, rays, different types of angles, and lines that are perpendicular, parallel, or intersecting.
Your child can draw shapes on a coordinate plane using points and find side lengths when points share an x or y coordinate.
Your child can solve problems by plotting points on a graph and find the perimeter or area of rectangles.
Your child can find the perimeter or area of a rectangle shown on a coordinate grid.
Your child can draw shapes on a graph using points and find side lengths when points share an x or y coordinate.
Your child can plot points on a grid to solve problems, draw shapes using points, and find side lengths of those shapes.
Your child can find the length of a side of a shape on a graph when the side is straight up/down or left/right.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Geometry — Distance, Perimeter, and Area on the Coordinate Plane
coherence_reason: All standards focus on plotting points/shapes on a coordinate grid and finding side lengths, perimeters, or areas, specifically when sides are horizontal or vertical.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find and place integers and rational numbers on number lines and on coordinate planes.
Your child can solve problems by plotting points in all four parts of the coordinate plane and use coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points.
Your child understands that rational numbers can be shown as points on number lines and coordinate planes, including negative numbers.
Your child can plot points on the first quadrant of a coordinate plane and explain what the points mean in real situations.
Your child can find and place integers and rational numbers on number lines and coordinate planes.
Your child can solve problems by graphing points in all four parts of the coordinate plane and find distances between points using coordinates and absolute value.
Your child can count the distance between two points that share the same x or y coordinate.
Your child can plot, order, and compare positive and negative whole numbers, fractions with the same bottom number, decimals to two places, and percentages all in the same form.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Rational Numbers — Number Line and Coordinate Plane Representation
coherence_reason: All standards focus on representing rational numbers (including integers and negatives) on number lines and coordinate planes, with some including applications like distance and real-world interpretation, but there is minor variation in emphasis (e.g., plotting, comparing, finding distances).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can understand inequalities as showing where numbers are positioned compared to each other on a number line.
Your child can tell the difference between comparing absolute values and comparing which number is bigger or smaller.
Your child can understand inequality statements as showing where two numbers are located on a number line.
Your child can turn a real-life situation into an inequality and show it on a number line.
Your child understands that inequalities show how two numbers are positioned on a number line.
Your child can identify whole numbers on a number line marked up to 50.
Your child can place three numbers that may include fractions or decimals on a number line.
Your child can find a missing whole number on a number line marked up to 50.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Number Lines — Inequalities and Number Placement
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on understanding and representing inequalities using number lines, but a few emphasize general number placement or identification, which is related but slightly broader.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can write inequalities like x > c or x < c to show limits in problems and draw their solutions on a number line.
Your child can write inequalities like x > 5 to show limits in problems and draw their solutions on a number line.
Your child can write or pick an inequality that shows a real-world situation.
Your child can write inequalities like x > c or x ≤ c to show limits or rules in problems and show all solutions on a number line.
Your child can write and show inequalities like x > 5 to describe limits in real-life or math problems and draw these on number lines.
Your child can write and understand inequalities like x > c or x ≤ c, explain when there are many solutions, and show these solutions on a number line.
Your child can write inequalities like x > c or x < c to describe limits or conditions in problems.
Your child understands that inequalities like x > c or x < c have many solutions.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Expressions and Equations — Writing and Graphing Inequalities
coherence_reason: All standards focus on writing inequalities to represent limits or conditions and graphing their solutions on a number line, with consistent emphasis on real-world and mathematical contexts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can pick the right math equation for a real-life problem where the missing number is one of the numbers being added, using a box to show the missing number.
Your child can think through and solve equations and inequalities that have one variable.
Your child understands that solving an equation or inequality means finding which numbers make it true and can check this by plugging in numbers.
Your child can pick the right math equation for a real-life problem where the answer is a total, using a box to show the missing number.
Your child can think through and solve equations and inequalities with one variable.
Your child understands that solving an equation or inequality means finding which numbers make it true and can check by substituting numbers.
Your child can solve one-step real-world equations using addition or subtraction with whole numbers.
Your child can solve one-step equations using fractions with the same bottom number or decimals from thousand to thousandths.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Expressions & Equations — One-Variable Equations and Inequalities
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding, formulating, and solving one-variable equations and inequalities, including real-world contexts and different number types.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can write, read, and find the value of expressions where letters stand for numbers.
Your child can use letters to stand for numbers and write expressions to solve problems, understanding that letters can mean unknown or any number in a group.
Your child writes arguments that support their main ideas with clear reasons, good evidence from trusted sources, and uses a formal writing style.
Your child can match a math equation to a real-life problem where letters or boxes stand for numbers.
Your child can use what they know about arithmetic to work with expressions that include variables.
Your child can write expressions that show math operations using numbers and letters for numbers.
Your child can write well-organized and detailed informative texts using precise language and a formal style.
Your child can write math expressions using numbers and letters that stand for numbers.

coherence_reason: Most standards are about writing, interpreting, and evaluating algebraic expressions, but two standards are about writing in English Language Arts, not mathematics.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can write and calculate expressions that include whole-number exponents.
Your child can find the value of expressions when numbers are plugged in, including using exponents and following the correct order of operations.
Your child can solve math expressions and formulas, including those with exponents and parentheses, like in real-life problems.
Your child understands and uses the chain rule to find derivatives of combined functions.
Your child finds derivatives of functions defined with parameters and uses implicit differentiation in various problems.
Your child can write and solve math expressions that include whole-number exponents.
Your child can break down whole numbers into prime factors with exponents.
Your child can solve math problems that use whole numbers with exponents.
canonical_name: Grade 6 Math: Expressions — Whole-Number Exponents and Order of Operations
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on evaluating and writing expressions with exponents and order of operations, but two standards about derivatives and differentiation are unrelated and indicate a second, advanced topic.
split_topics: Expressions with exponents and order of operations
